name: McNdroid
alternateName: McNdroid: A Longitudinal Multimodal Benchmark for Robust Drift Detection in Android Malware
description: McNdroid is a large-scale, longitudinal, multimodal Android malware detection dataset designed for concept-drift evaluation. It spans samples collected from 2013 to 2025 and provides three aligned modalities: static feature vectors, API call graph/GML features, and JSON-derived behavioral features. Rows across modalities are aligned by sample hash and binary label; the associated loader validates that data, GML, and JSON rows have identical hash order and identical labels before training.

distribution.name: metadata.csv
distribution.description: Sample-level metadata including SHA256 hashes, collection timestamps, labels, and source information.

distribution.name: vendor_family_wide_verdict.csv
distribution.description: Multi-vendor malware family verdicts for label-noise, family attribution, and multi-label learning research.

distribution.name: data-train-feature-files
distribution.description: Sparse SciPy NPZ feature matrices for the static data modality training split.

distribution.name: data-train-meta-files
distribution.description: NumPy NPZ metadata files for the static data modality training split. Each file contains arrays y and hash.

distribution.name: data-test-feature-files
distribution.description: Sparse SciPy NPZ feature matrices for yearly static data modality test splits.

distribution.name: data-test-meta-files
distribution.description: NumPy NPZ metadata files for yearly static data modality test splits. Each file contains arrays y and hash.

distribution.name: gml-train-files
distribution.description: Dense NumPy NPZ files for the GML/API call graph training split. Each file contains arrays X, y, and hash.

distribution.name: gml-test-files
distribution.description: Dense NumPy NPZ files for yearly GML/API call graph test splits. Each file contains arrays X, y, and hash.

distribution.name: json-train-feature-files
distribution.description: Sparse SciPy NPZ feature matrices for the JSON modality training split. The JSON modality uses an additional nested year directory.

distribution.name: json-train-meta-files
distribution.description: NumPy NPZ metadata files for the JSON modality training split. Each file contains arrays y and hashes.

distribution.name: json-test-feature-files
distribution.description: Sparse SciPy NPZ feature matrices for yearly JSON modality test splits. The JSON modality uses an additional nested year directory.

distribution.name: json-test-meta-files
distribution.description: NumPy NPZ metadata files for yearly JSON modality test splits. Each file contains arrays y and hashes.

rai:dataLimitations: McNdroid is intended as a research benchmark for longitudinal Android malware detection, multimodal learning, and concept-drift evaluation; it is not a deployment-ready malware detection system and should not be used as the sole basis for operational security decisions, automated app takedown, or attribution. The dataset represents Android application samples available to the creators and their collection pipeline from 2013 to 2025, so it may not cover all malware families, benign app categories, regional app markets, packer/obfuscation techniques, or post-2025 threats. Labels and family information may inherit noise, inconsistency, or temporal drift from vendor verdicts. Feature modalities are limited to static feature vectors, API call graph/GML features, and JSON-derived behavioral/static-analysis features; dynamic runtime behavior, network traces, user interaction traces, and device-specific effects are not comprehensively represented. Benchmark results obtained on McNdroid should therefore be interpreted as evidence about robustness under the dataset's specified temporal splits and modalities, not as proof of real-world detection performance across all Android ecosystems.
rai:dataBiases: The dataset may contain temporal, source, and labeling biases. Because samples span 2013 to 2025, older years may overrepresent historical malware behaviors, APIs, packaging conventions, and benign app ecosystems, while newer threats or apps distributed outside the collection sources may be underrepresented. Source bias may arise from the repositories, feeds, or analysis pipeline used to obtain Android application samples and extract features. Vendor-family verdicts can introduce label bias because anti-malware vendors may disagree, update signatures over time, use different naming conventions, or detect some malware families more reliably than others. The binary malware/benign labels may simplify ambiguous cases such as potentially unwanted applications, grayware, repackaged apps, or samples with partial evidence. Modality-specific extraction failures or parser limitations may disproportionately affect obfuscated, packed, malformed, or unusually structured APKs, which can bias models toward samples that are easier to analyze.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: The released metadata and features are not intended to contain direct personally identifiable information such as names, email addresses, phone numbers, government identifiers, faces, or user account data. The dataset does include SHA256-style application sample hashes, collection timestamps, malware/benign labels, source information, vendor/family verdicts, and extracted Android application features. These elements are security-research metadata rather than human-subject attributes, but they may indirectly relate to software publishers, app provenance, or regional app ecosystems depending on the original sample sources. No sensitive human attributes such as gender, age, health or medical data, political or religious beliefs, socio-economic status, or direct geographic location of individuals are intentionally included. Users should still avoid attempting to re-identify developers, users, or organizations from hashes, source metadata, package artifacts, or vendor-family labels.
rai:dataUseCases: McNdroid is designed to represent longitudinal Android malware detection under temporal concept drift using aligned multimodal features. Validated or intended research use cases include benchmarking Android malware classification across year-based train/test splits; evaluating robustness to concept drift from 2013 to 2025; studying multimodal fusion across static feature vectors, API call graph/GML features, and JSON-derived features; testing continual learning, domain adaptation, and drift-detection methods; and analyzing label noise or family attribution using the vendor_family_wide_verdict.csv table. The metadata states that rows across modalities are aligned by sample hash and binary label and that the associated loader validates identical hash order and labels before training. Use cases not validated include production malware blocking, legal or disciplinary attribution, commercial threat-intelligence claims, detection of non-Android malware, privacy or user-behavior profiling, and claims about fairness across human demographic groups.
rai:dataSocialImpact: The dataset can benefit cybersecurity research by enabling reproducible evaluation of Android malware detectors under temporal drift, supporting more robust and transparent defenses against evolving mobile threats. It can also help researchers compare multimodal and continual-learning methods under a common benchmark. Potential negative impacts include overclaiming operational readiness from benchmark performance, reinforcing vendor-label errors, misclassifying benign or grayware applications, or using family labels for unsupported attribution. There is also dual-use risk: malware feature datasets can inform both defensive research and adversarial adaptation by revealing which feature representations detectors study. Mitigations include documenting limitations and intended use, requiring users to treat the dataset as a research benchmark rather than a deployment authority, avoiding release of direct user data, and encouraging independent validation on additional contemporary and operational datasets before any real-world security use.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: Android application sample and metadata collection
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Collection of Android application samples and sample-level metadata spanning 2013 to 2025, including hash identifiers, collection timestamps, labels, and source information recorded in metadata.csv. The collection method is based on existing Android application/security datasets, repositories, feeds, or sample sources available to the dataset creators; downstream users should consult the dataset repository and metadata.csv for source-specific details.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: IQSeC Lab
prov:wasAttributedTo.description: Research organization responsible for curating and releasing the McNdroid dataset.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: Md Mahmuduzzaman Kamol
prov:wasAttributedTo.description: Dataset creator/contributor listed in the Croissant metadata.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: Malware label and family verdict aggregation
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Aggregation of binary malware/benign labels and multi-vendor malware family verdicts. vendor_family_wide_verdict.csv records per-vendor family-level verdicts for label-noise, family attribution, and multi-label learning research. Labels and family names may reflect vendor-specific naming conventions and detection policies.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: IQSeC Lab
prov:wasAttributedTo.description: Research team responsible for integrating labels and vendor-family verdict metadata.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: Vendor verdict aggregation pipeline
prov:wasAttributedTo.description: Automated or semi-automated pipeline used to consolidate malware labels and family verdict records.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: Multimodal feature extraction and preprocessing
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Extraction and preprocessing of three aligned modalities: sparse static feature vectors under data_feature/processed_data, dense API call graph/GML feature arrays under gml_feature/processed_data, and sparse JSON-derived feature vectors under json_feature/processed_data. The released files include SciPy sparse matrices and NumPy NPZ arrays split by initialization year and test year.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: IQSeC Lab
prov:wasAttributedTo.description: Research team responsible for feature engineering, preprocessing, and release packaging.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: McNdroid loader and preprocessing pipeline
prov:wasAttributedTo.description: Software pipeline used to construct feature matrices, metadata arrays, and year-based train/test splits.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: Cross-modal alignment and quality validation
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Validation that rows across static data, GML/API call graph, and JSON-derived modalities are aligned by sample hash and binary label. The associated loader checks that data, GML, and JSON rows have identical hash order and identical labels before training.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: McNdroid loader validation
prov:wasAttributedTo.description: Software checks for cross-modal hash-order and label consistency before model training.

prov:wasAttributedTo.prov:label: IQSeC Lab
prov:wasAttributedTo.description: Research team responsible for quality review and release validation.
